front: Poem: 'They fuck you up, your mum and dad'; 'Man hands on misery to man. / It deepens like a coastal shelf'; advises 'don't have any kids yourself'
Poem: an earl and countess 'lie in stone' in Chichester Cathedral; 'Time has transfigured them into / Untruth'; concludes 'What will survive of us is love' — an 'almost-instinct almost truth'
Poem: speaker visits an empty church, donates an Irish sixpence, reads 'Here endeth' too loudly; 'A serious house on serious earth'; calls religion a 'vast moth-eaten musical brocade'
Poem: speaker rides a 'three-quarters-empty train' on a sunlit Saturday, observing bridal parties at stations; ends with 'a sense of falling, like an arrow-shower'
Poem: imagines 'everyone young going down the long slide to happiness, endlessly'; ends with 'the deep blue air, that shows / Nothing, and is nowhere, and is endless'
Poem: opens 'I work all day, and get half-drunk at night'; describes death as 'The anaesthetic from which none come round'; 'postmen like doctors go from house to house'
Poem: 'Sexual intercourse began / In nineteen sixty-three / Between the end of the Chatterley ban / And the Beatles' first LP'
Poem: speaker rents the room of the deceased title character; reflects 'how we live measures our own nature'; 'no more to show / Than one hired box'
